facts: Enlightenment thinkers like John Locke argued that people have natural rights to life, liberty, and property, which governments cannot violate.
Jean-Jacques Rousseau claimed that legitimate government requires the consent of the governed, not rule by divine right or force.
The American Revolution (1776) was directly inspired by Enlightenment ideas, especially Locke's natural rights, as seen in the Declaration of Independence.
The French Revolution (1789) was also inspired by Enlightenment ideas, but it went further by abolishing the monarchy and attempting to create a republic based on equality.
Napoleon spread revolutionary ideals like equality before the law and career open to talent across Europe through his conquests, even though he later made himself emperor.
The Latin American revolutions (1810–1825) were led by Creole elites who were influenced by Enlightenment ideas and the success of the American and French revolutions.
Montesquieu argued for separation of powers among executive, legislative, and judicial branches to prevent tyranny, a principle later built into the U.S. Constitution.
misconceptions: Students think the American Revolution was only about taxation and had no connection to Enlightenment philosophy, but the Declaration of Independence explicitly uses Locke's natural rights argument.
Students believe Napoleon completely rejected Enlightenment ideals because he crowned himself emperor, but he actually spread equality before the law and merit-based advancement across Europe.

Students assume Latin American revolutions were populist uprisings led by the common people, when in fact they were led by Creole elites who wanted to take power from Spanish officials while keeping their own social standing.
